symbols: Stones being hurled — the instrument of execution under Jewish law for blasphemy
Stephen kneeling or fallen — submission to martyrdom, imitating Christ's surrender
Hostile crowd — the religious authorities and mob who rejected Stephen's testimony
Open sky and landscape — the heavenly vision Stephen received before death
description: A crowd of figures in turbans and robes gather on an open hillside, some hurling stones at a central fallen figure. The attackers adopt vigorous throwing postures, arms raised with stones. In the background, a city with walls and towers is visible beneath dramatic clouds. Mountains rise in the distance. The scene is filled with violent action and agitation among the participants, with garments billowing in movement.
theological_description: The stoning of Stephen, recorded in Acts 7, is the first martyrdom of the Christian Church. Stephen, 'a man full of faith and of the Holy Spirit' (ACT.6.5), was brought before the Sanhedrin for his bold proclamation of Jesus as the Christ. His lengthy speech recounting Israel's history of rejecting God's messengers culminated in the accusation: 'You stiff-necked people... you always resist the Holy Spirit' (ACT.7.51). As they stoned him, Stephen saw 'the heavens opened, and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God' (ACT.7.56). His dying words echo Christ's own from the cross: 'Lord, do not hold this sin against them' (ACT.7.60; cf. LUK.23.34). The Fathers saw Stephen as the perfect imitator of Christ — accused falsely, forgiving his killers, commending his spirit to God. Significantly, Saul of Tarsus was present, approving the execution (ACT.8.1), making Stephen's martyrdom a pivotal link in the chain leading to Paul's conversion and the Gentile mission.

scripture_refs.reason: The stoning of Stephen, the specific event depicted in the painting
Stephen's vision of the heavens opened and the Son of Man at God's right hand
Stephen chosen as one of the seven deacons, full of faith and the Holy Spirit
Christ's prayer 'Father, forgive them' paralleled by Stephen's dying prayer for his persecutors
Saul's approval of Stephen's death, connecting this martyrdom to the future apostle Paul
